_comment: Example content.json authored by Claude for the pdt-orbitpay demo account. Every string is Claude-written narrative, not a template extraction. Edit any field and re-run renderer.js to update the deck.

slide2_environment.subtitle: pdt-orbitpay  ·  90-day analysis window  ·  Operations Cloud Ultimate
slide2_environment.context_bar: Plan: Operations Cloud Ultimate  ·  Priorities: P1–P5  ·  25 Event Orchestrations  ·  AIOps alert grouping on 100 services
slide2_environment.teaser: The actual migration scope is much smaller than these headline numbers — the next slide explains why.

slide3_scope.subtitle: Services with incidents in the last 90 days vs. services that haven't fired in over three months
slide3_scope.active_desc: 268 services have had at least one incident in the last 90 days — these are the services your engineers actually respond to.
slide3_scope.stale_desc: 404 services haven't fired a single incident in 90 days. These aren't a migration problem — they're a service catalog cleanup opportunity you've been meaning to do anyway.
slide3_scope.stale_note: 60% stale is high even for a large payments platform. Sprint 0 will validate whether any stale services are silently critical before we lock the scope.
slide3_scope.summary_bar: Real migration scope: 268 active services across 61 teams — the 404 stale services are candidates for decommission, not migration

slide4_teams.subtitle: Teams are the migration unit — each wave migrates 10–20 teams together, validated before the next wave begins
slide4_teams.footer: Recommended pilot: Release Engineering + Business Operations — 15 active services combined, no ServiceNow dependency, and straightforward on-call structure. Start here, stabilise, then expand.

slide5_shadow.subtitle: Integrations and workflows outside the core migration path — each one has a well-understood migration pattern

rows.label: ServiceNow
rows.description: Workflow-driven (P1/P2 only via 'ServiceNOW INC for P1 and P2'). This is actually the easiest SN migration pattern — it migrates as a single incident.io Workflow action. Sprint 3 is dedicated to validating bidirectional sync before any SN-dependent teams move.

rows.label: Complex Incident Workflows
rows.description: 'Declare Major Incident' (15 steps) and 'ops-srmr' (14 steps). Both rebuild in incident.io's Workflow engine. Sprint 5 is reserved for these specifically — incident commanders sign off before any P1 process is considered migrated.

rows.label: Unknown Webhook Destinations
rows.description: 112 webhook subscriptions point to eventsender-f81fbd33135c.herokuapp.com. This is the single biggest unknown in the migration — Sprint 0 identifies whether it's a live integration or legacy noise before we lock the sprint plan.

rows.label: Automation Actions
rows.description: 14 automation actions including Aurora DB checks, CX QA diagnostics, and Instant Transfer enrichment. These migrate to incident.io Runbooks. Sprint 7 is dedicated to this — Runbook runner infrastructure needs to be confirmed beforehand.

rows.label: Collaboration Tools
rows.description: ✓ All three detected. incident.io has native first-class integrations for Slack, Teams, and Zoom — not webhook adapters. This actually simplifies migration: PD's webhook-based integrations get replaced by tighter, purpose-built ones.

rows.label: Status Pages
rows.description: ✓ No public status pages. 4 internal stakeholder dashboards only — no subscriber list migration needed. incident.io Status Pages covers these without an additional licence.

rows.label: Live Call Routing
rows.description: ✓ Not detected. Live Call Routing has no direct equivalent in incident.io, so its absence removes a potential migration blocker entirely.

slide6_sprint.subtitle: 268 active services  ·  61 active teams  ·  ServiceNow (workflow-driven)  ·  2 complex workflows  ·  14 automation actions  ·  112 unknown webhooks

sprints.label: Sprint 0
sprints.milestone: incident.io environment live; eventsender webhook identified; ServiceNow approach confirmed; sprint plan locked

sprints.label: Sprint 1
sprints.milestone: First 15 services live on incident.io; dual-run stable; on-call engineers validated end-to-end

sprints.label: Sprint 2
sprints.milestone: ~12 teams migrated; ~60 active services on incident.io; no PD dependency for migrated teams

sprints.label: Sprint 3
sprints.milestone: P1/P2 → ServiceNow sync validated bidirectionally; SN-dependent teams cleared to migrate

sprints.label: Sprint 4
sprints.milestone: ~24 teams on incident.io; Risk & Compliance, Identity & Access migrated

sprints.label: Sprint 5
sprints.milestone: 'Declare Major Incident' and 'ops-srmr' rebuilt and signed off by incident commanders

sprints.label: Sprint 6
sprints.milestone: Data & Analytics (20 svc) and Developer Platform (16 svc) migrated; ~40 teams on incident.io

sprints.label: Sprint 7
sprints.milestone: All 14 automation actions live as incident.io Runbooks; runner infrastructure validated

sprints.label: Sprint 8
sprints.milestone: Core Infrastructure, Customer Experience, Security Ops migrated; ~50 teams on incident.io

sprints.label: Sprint 9
sprints.milestone: All 61 active teams migrated; eventsender webhook resolved or retired; 268 services on incident.io

sprints.label: Sprint 10
sprints.milestone: PagerDuty routing disabled. incident.io is the primary platform. Hypercare begins.

slide6_sprint.summary_note: Final timeline confirmed in Sprint 0 once the eventsender webhook is understood

easy_items.headline: Events API v2 integrations
easy_items.detail: Your alert sources use the modern Events API v2 — clean, direct migration to incident.io. No payload translation needed.

easy_items.headline: Slack + Teams + Zoom — all native
easy_items.detail: incident.io treats all three as first-class integrations. PD's webhook adapters get replaced with tighter, purpose-built connections.

easy_items.headline: No Live Call Routing
easy_items.detail: LCR has no direct equivalent in incident.io — its absence removes one of the most common migration blockers we encounter.

easy_items.headline: 25 Event Orchestrations
easy_items.detail: incident.io Alert Routes are a direct equivalent for your 25 event orchestrations. Routing logic maps cleanly without redesign.

easy_items.headline: P1–P5 severity model
easy_items.detail: Your five priority levels map directly to incident.io Severity. No severity model redesign needed — names, colours, and descriptions carry over.

discussion_items.headline: 112 unknown webhook subscriptions
discussion_items.detail: 112 webhooks point to eventsender-f81fbd33135c.herokuapp.com. Is this a live integration or old test infrastructure? Sprint 0 answers this before any migration scope is locked.

discussion_items.headline: 'Declare Major Incident' workflow (15 steps)
discussion_items.detail: This is your most important process rebuild. We need to walk through it step by step with incident commanders before Sprint 5 to surface any hidden dependencies.

discussion_items.headline: ServiceNow — who owns it?
discussion_items.detail: The SN integration is workflow-driven (P1/P2 only) — good news on complexity. But Sprint 3 needs the ITSM team in the room, not just the PD admin.

discussion_items.headline: Automation runner infrastructure
discussion_items.detail: 14 automation actions including DB checks and auto-remediation scripts. We need to confirm what infrastructure the runner has access to before Sprint 7 starts.

discussion_items.headline: 100 services on AIOps grouping
discussion_items.detail: Are engineers actively relying on intelligent grouping, or did it come on by default? This affects how we position incident.io's noise reduction in the value story.

slide8_questions.subtitle: Tailored to OrbitPay's environment — use as a call guide and check off as you cover them

questions.question: "What happens if PagerDuty goes down for 4 hours?"
questions.why: Always surfaces shadow backup processes and undocumented escalation paths that won't appear in any API export. Never fails to reveal something useful.

questions.question: "Of your 672 services, how many actually page your engineers week to week?"
questions.why: Validates the 268 active count and often reveals that the real operational scope is even smaller — important before the scope reframe lands.

questions.question: "What's hitting eventsender-f81fbd33135c.herokuapp.com? Is that a live integration?"
questions.why: 112 webhook subscriptions point there. If it's live, it's a migration workstream. If it's legacy noise, we remove it from scope and compress Sprint 9.

questions.question: "Which team is most excited about moving to incident.io?"
questions.why: Identifies your pilot wave champion. The right first team makes the rollout contagious. The wrong one creates drag that slows every subsequent wave.

questions.question: "Who owns the ServiceNow integration — your PD admin or the ITSM team?"
questions.why: Sprint 3 needs the right person in the room. Wrong stakeholder in Sprint 3 = blocked sprint, delayed SN-dependent team migrations, timeline slip.

questions.question: "Can we walk through the 'Declare Major Incident' workflow step by step?"
questions.why: A 15-step workflow rebuilt from memory always misses something. Sprint 5 goes much smoother if incident commanders have co-authored the spec in advance.

questions.question: "Is your PagerDuty contract up for renewal in the next 12 months?"
questions.why: A contract deadline is the single most effective forcing function for timeline compression. If it exists, it changes the parallel wave conversation entirely.

questions.question: "Are your engineers actively using the intelligent alert grouping, or did it come on by default?"
questions.why: 100 services have AIOps grouping configured. If engineers rely on it daily, that's a strong incident.io selling point. If it's default-on, it's noise in the value story.

reasons.title: AI SRE Agent — included, not add-on
reasons.body: Your 100 services with intelligent alert grouping map directly to incident.io's AI noise reduction. Same capability — no separate AIOps licence to negotiate.

reasons.title: Slack + Teams + Zoom, natively
reasons.body: You're already on all three. incident.io treats them as first-class integrations — not the webhook adapters PD uses. Fewer moving parts in your incident response stack.

reasons.title: Workflow engine built for complexity
reasons.body: Your two 'Declare Major Incident' workflows (15 and 14 steps) rebuild cleanly. Conditions, branching, ServiceNow triggers, and multi-step automation are all native.

reasons.title: Runbooks replace Automation Actions
reasons.body: Your 14 PD Automation Actions — including Aurora DB checks and CX QA diagnostics — migrate to incident.io Runbooks with tighter incident context and better audit trails.

reasons.title: Status Pages included
reasons.body: Your 4 internal stakeholder dashboards migrate to incident.io Status Pages at no extra cost. PagerDuty charges premium for status page functionality.

reasons.title: Migrations complete faster than you expect
reasons.body: 7shifts: 2 weeks (70 engineers). Trustly: 6 weeks (200 users). When a contract date creates urgency, teams find a way. The parallel wave option exists if you need it.

steps.title: Sprint 0 Kickoff  (1 week)
bullets: Get ServiceNow ITSM team contact for Sprint 3
Final sprint plan locked with scope confirmed

steps.title: Pilot Wave  (3 weeks)
bullets: Alert → incident → escalation → resolution validated end-to-end
Dual-run: PD stays live for all other teams

steps.title: Full Migration  (~9 more sprints)
bullets: 268 active services across 61 teams on incident.io
ServiceNow, major incident workflows, automation in dedicated sprints

slide10_next_steps.cta: Book a kickoff call  ·  incident.io/demo  ·  or reply to this deck to get started
